history: The patient is involved in a complex romantic situation with Cleo, who is simultaneously in a long-distance relationship with someone else. This dual involvement creates ongoing uncertainty and emotional strain for the patient. The patient often finds themselves providing emotional support to Cleo, even when their own needs are not being addressed. There is a pattern of the patient prioritizing Cleo's needs, potentially at the expense of their own self-care, and feeling emotionally exhausted by the situation.
unlovable_belief: I am bound to be abandoned
I am bound to be rejected
intermediate_belief: If I don't support Cleo emotionally, she will not value me or might distance herself, so I must always be there for her even if it means neglecting my own needs. My worth in this relationship depends on how much I can help her. I can't risk expressing my own needs because it might push her away.
intermediate_belief_depression: No matter how much I support Cleo, it will never be enough for her to choose me, so there's no point in hoping things will change for me.
coping_strategies: The patient seeks reassurance from Cleo or others about their relationship status and withdraws when feeling overwhelmed. They avoid discussing the future of the relationship to prevent conflict and often prioritize Cleo's needs over their own, neglecting self-care and risking emotional exhaustion.
situation: During an outing, Cleo discussed relationship problems and sought emotional support.
auto_thought: I'm just the person Cleo turns to when she has problems, not someone she truly wants to be with. If I don't help her now, she'll stop needing me and I'll lose her.
behavior: I listen closely to Cleo's problems and offer her emotional support. I hold back my own feelings and avoid bringing up my concerns about our relationship. I focus on making sure she feels better, even if I feel drained.
